title: Rāmāyaṇa
author: Vālmīki
source_metadata_summary: dcs_scope_hint=Sanskrit Epic Literature; dcs_subject=Ramayana; dcs_time_slot=epic; dcs_author=Valmīki; dcs_completed=false
classification_notes: The Rāmāyaṇa, traditionally ascribed to the poet Vālmīki, is a foundational epic of Indian civilization and the earliest surviving Sanskrit kāvya (ādikāvya). It narrates the exile of Rāma, the abduction of Sītā, and the war to rescue her, establishing ideals of dharma, kingship, and devotion. Its influence pervades literature, art, religion, and ethics across South and Southeast Asia, making it one of the most central texts in the entire Sanskrit corpus.

title: Carakasaṃhitā
author: Caraka
classification_notes: The Carakasaṃhitā, attributed to Caraka and redacted by Dṛḍhabala, is the foundational text of Ayurvedic internal medicine (kāyacikitsā). Encompassing eight major sections, it covers etiology, diagnosis, treatment, pharmacy, and regimen, integrating philosophical concepts with empirical observation. It remains a living authority in Ayurveda and a key source for the history of Indian medicine.

title: Mūlamadhyamakārikāḥ
author: Nāgārjuna
source_metadata_summary: dcs_scope_hint=Buddhist Scripture; dcs_subject=Buddhist; dcs_time_slot=epic; dcs_author=Nāgārjuna; dcs_completed=false
classification_notes: Nāgārjuna's Mūlamadhyamakārikāḥ (Fundamental Verses on the Middle Way) is the foundational text of the Madhyamaka school of Buddhist philosophy. In 27 chapters of terse verse, it deconstructs all notions of inherent existence (svabhāva) and establishes the doctrine of emptiness (śūnyatā) through rigorous dialectics. One of the most studied and commented-upon works in the entire Buddhist canon, it profoundly influenced all later Mahāyāna traditions.

title: Yogasūtra
author: Patañjali
source_metadata_summary: dcs_scope_hint=Sanskrit Philosophy; dcs_subject=Yoga; dcs_time_slot=epic; dcs_author=Patañjali; dcs_completed=false
classification_notes: Patañjali's Yogasūtra is the foundational text of the Yoga philosophical tradition, presenting the eight-limbed path (aṣṭāṅga-yoga) in 195 succinct sūtras across four chapters. It systematizes earlier yogic practices and psychology, and is arguably the most widely known work of Indian philosophy globally. Its influence spans classical darśana, medieval yoga traditions, modern yoga practice, and global spirituality.

title: Bhagavadgītā
author: Vyāsa
classification_notes: The Bhagavadgītā, traditionally attributed to Vyāsa, is a 700-verse dialogue within the Mahābhārata (Bhīṣmaparvan 23–40). It addresses dharma, yoga, bhakti, and Vedānta metaphysics through the conversation between Kṛṣṇa and Arjuna on the eve of battle. As a standalone text, it is the most widely read and commented-upon philosophical poem in the Sanskrit corpus, foundational for Hindu theology and Indian philosophy.
